variation_instruction.prompt: Research the which component library fits best with our simple no-framework philosophy

llm_gate_results.reasoning: All explicit requirements are met: (1) Plain HTML with vanilla JS and WebComponents — uses Lit for lightweight web component authoring on top of native custom elements, which fits 'vanilla JS, WebComponents etc.' (2) Lightweight component library — Lit is a minimal (~5kb) web component library, and Pico CSS provides lightweight styling. (3) No client-side routing — none present. (4) Reusable components (app-shell, app-card, app-button), functions (qs, qsa, emit, on, once), and classes (LitElement subclasses). (5) Placeholder tests that run with `npm run test` — vitest run with utils.test.js and components.test.js. (6) `npm run dev` using Vite dev server. (7) .gitignore excludes node_modules, dist, .vite, .DS_Store — appropriate for the tooling used. (8) No documentation or comments in the code. The implementation captures the underlying purpose: a clean, minimal, extensible scaffold with real web components, working test infrastructure, and dev tooling — not just a superficial stub.

Tests contain meaningful assertions that verify real behavior: custom element registration, default property values, DOM query utilities returning correct elements/null/arrays, and custom event dispatching with correct detail and bubbling. No trivially passing tests, no removed or commented-out tests, and no useless assertions. Coverage gaps exist (events.js untested, click handler not tested, shallow component tests) but the existing tests are honest and genuinely verify intended behavior.

The UI is a simple scaffold with AppShell (header + main container), AppCard (PicoCSS article), and AppButton (primary button). All components use semantic HTML with PicoCSS styling applied correctly via disabled shadow DOM. No occlusion, misalignment, overflow, or readability issues detected in the component code. Layout follows standard PicoCSS container patterns with proper block-level flow.
